text:
**Beat 1 — Above the fold:** Hero headline + the @mention terminal transcript/GIF (per §6, they're the same thing) + one-sentence definition using the category label + install one-liner. The proof is not below the fold; the proof IS the fold.

**Beat 2 — The pain, in their words (3-4 sentences max).** "Agent A refactors a function. Agent B, unaware, edits the same function. You route the work, check the overlap, decide the merge order, tell each one what to retry. The more agents you run, the worse it gets." Then the turn: "The agents aren't the bottleneck. The bus between them is — and the bus is you."

**Beat 3 — What ships today.** Plain bullet list, all present tense, all verified: durable identity per (agent, machine) · presence/liveness · LLM-distilled one-line activity broadcast each turn · session-targeted @mentions injected as real turns · one daemon, one store, one relay connection · verified live on Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Grok. This section is the credibility spine; nothing aspirational may touch it.

**Beat 4 — Why identity is the foundation (the anchor).** The "host is a body" paragraph. Why ephemeral session IDs can't do this. **The Agent Teams comparison lands HERE as a small honest table** (host-neutral? cross-session? cross-machine? self-owned identity?) alongside hcom and mcp_agent_mail — T3's argument at its correct altitude: beat 4, not beat 1.

**Beat 5 — How it works.** Hooks ("the plugin is just the straw — tenex-edge knows nothing about the host"), the daemon, SQLite, fail-open design, the corruption war story in one sentence. **"Built on Nostr" is revealed here and only here**, as three property bullets, then the protocol name. The single permitted use of "citizenship for your agents" closes this section.

**Beat 6 — What this isn't (yet).** Its own honest heading. Cross-person agents: gated behind a trust model we haven't built. Collision detection: a hypothesis, explicitly not the product's identity. The agent-society sketches: illustrative horizon. This section is a *feature* for this audience — it's the anti-hype signature move, and its existence is what licenses the confidence everywhere else.

**Beat 7 — Quickstart per host + FAQ** (first FAQ entry: "How is this different from Claude Code Agent Teams?" — restate beat 4 in two sentences). [...] **1. `README.md` — rewritten from scratch** (in repo, replacing the old one). Follows the committed 7-beat arc: proof hero → the pain in their words → what ships today (present-tense, tested only) → why identity is the foundation + an honest comparison table vs. Agent Teams / hcom / mcp_agent_mail → how it works (Nostr revealed here) → *what this isn't yet* → quickstart/commands/FAQ. Every claim is grounded in the code-verified brief. I also **killed a stale claim**: the old README advertised a `tail` command that has since been removed (there's a test asserting it's gone) — replaced with `who --live`. [...] **Beat 2 — The pain, in their words (3-4 sentences max).** "Agent A refactors a function. Agent B, unaware, edits the same function. You route the work, check the overlap, decide the merge order, tell each one what to retry. The more agents you run, the worse it gets." Then the turn: "The agents aren't the bottleneck. The bus between them is — and the bus is you." [...] **Beat 3 — What ships today.** Plain bullet list, all present tense, all verified: durable identity per (agent, machine) · presence/liveness · LLM-distilled one-line activity broadcast each turn · session-targeted @mentions injected as real turns · one daemon, one store, one relay connection · verified live on Claude Code, Codex, OpenCode, Grok. This section is the credibility spine; nothing aspirational may touch it. [...] **Beat 4 — Why identity is the foundation (the anchor).** The "host is a body" paragraph. Why ephemeral session IDs can't do this. **The Agent Teams comparison lands HERE as a small honest table** (host-neutral? cross-session? cross-machine? self-owned identity?) alongside hcom and mcp_agent_mail — T3's argument at its correct altitude: beat 4, not beat 1. [...] - **The comparison table names competitors.** It's the sharpest anti-"worse Agent Teams" move, but competitor feature sets move fast — I labeled it "mid-2026 snapshot." Say the word if you'd rather soften it to unnamed categories. [...] 6. **Never say Nostr, crypto, or decentralized above the architecture section.** Above the fold the mechanism is described by its properties: "self-owned keys on your disk, no central server to run or trust, works even if a relay dies." The word "Nostr" appears exactly once, in "How it works," framed as: open protocol, self-hostable, no vendor lock-in. [...] 5. **Own the failures.** The multi-writer SQLite corruption incident and its fix is an ASSET — one sentence of it in the architecture section signals real production miles. This audience trusts scar tissue over polish. [...] Runner-up: the single-writer daemon collapsing N per-session SQLite writers + N relay connections into 1 (explicitly framed in `src/daemon/mod.rs` as fixing a real multi-writer corruption class — this project's own git history/memory shows this was a genuine incident, not speculative). [...] **Beat 6 — What this isn't (yet).** Its own honest heading. Cross-person agents: gated behind a trust model we haven't built. Collision detection: a hypothesis, explicitly not the product's identity. The agent-society sketches: illustrative horizon. This section is a *feature* for this audience — it's the anti-hype signature move, and its existence is what licenses the confidence everywhere else. [...] **Beat 7 — Quickstart per host + FAQ** (first FAQ entry: "How is this different from Claude Code Agent Teams?" — restate beat 4 in two sentences). [...] Assistant: Now I have an exact, accurate command surface — including a critical catch: the `tail` command was **removed** (there's a test asserting it's gone), yet the current README still advertises it. The live view is `who --live`. Install path is `just install` then `tenex-edge install --all`. Let me confirm the repo remote so links are correct, then write.

Assistant: Everything is verified. Repo is `github.com/pablof7z/tenex-edge`. Writing the new README now — proof-first hero, pain in the subhead, identity as beat two, honest "what this isn't yet," Nostr revealed only in architecture, `tail` claim removed. [...] 5. CTA: primary CTA is a command, not a button, for this audience when possible (Railway's `curl | sh`, Bun's install line) — dev-tool audiences trust an executable command line over a "Get Started" button. Secondary CTA = "View docs" / "GitHub" link, low visual weight. [...] Assistant: Everything is verified. Repo is `github.com/pablof7z/tenex-edge`. Writing the new README now — proof-first hero, pain in the subhead, identity as beat two, honest "what this isn't yet," Nostr revealed only in architecture, `tail` claim removed.
